edges.explanation: NATO agreeing 5% at The Hague while UK is at 2.3% proves the 2.5% target is already obsolete
The gap between 2.3% current and 5% target quantifies the trade-off Spain honestly named
Doubling spending to £130bn/yr cannot happen without either massive tax increases or devastating cuts to other services
£130bn/yr vs £60bn current makes the welfare state incompatibility argument arithmetically concrete
The £70bn additional for 5% is the largest single component of the £80-130bn total unfunded gap
50,000 fewer personnel since 2010 while at 15% below target proves the capital-heavy bias has hollowed out the force
Personnel decline directly justifies the rebalance toward people alongside technology
Capital up 95.5% while day-to-day fell 2.4% is the direct measurement of the imbalance the evidence identifies
The spending composition data directly justifies the rebalance policy
Germany outspending the UK for the first time with a 24% increase proves the 2.5% target leaves the UK falling behind allies
OBR showing 3% costs £17.3bn/yr more with non-defence spending 'almost flat' proves service crowding is real
£17.3bn additional for 3% alongside flat non-defence spending is the specific evidence of the fiscal gap
Spain's PM explicitly stating 5% is incompatible with a welfare state is the single most honest framing of the trade-off
£6.1bn from aid cuts provides immediate funding but the military's own argument that aid is security investment supports the concern about long-term risk
The military's own argument that aid is upstream security investment justifies maintaining the 0.5% commitment
2.5% being obsolete forces the question Spain answered honestly — how much defence can coexist with public services
The capital-personnel imbalance being wrong directly supports the rebalance policy
Defence increases requiring tax rises proves the welfare state trade-off is real and unaddressed
The inability to fund defence without cutting services or raising taxes is a core component of the £80-130bn total gap
Aid cuts creating new security risks supports maintaining aid as a complementary security investment
Strong strategic case in current threat environment directly supports maintaining the deterrent
The defence spending pathway depends on honestly confronting the welfare state trade-off that Spain named
The defence spending policy depends on the £80-130bn gap being addressed through taxation reform not service cuts
Nuclear deterrent maintenance depends on the strategic case remaining strong
Rebalancing depends on the finding that the current capital-heavy bias has hollowed out conventional capability
Maintaining aid depends on the finding that cutting it creates new security risks that offset the funding gain
Drone manufacturing depends on the overall fiscal constraint making cheap asymmetric capability more valuable than expensive traditional platforms
2.5% by 2030 is the right direction but already obsolete given NATO's 5% agreement at The Hague
Accelerated 2.5% and pathway to 3% is closer to the evidence-based minimum than any other party
Nuclear deterrent commitment directly matches the evidence-based position
Cutting aid from 0.5% to 0.3% directly contradicts the policy of maintaining aid as upstream security investment
2.5% commitment is necessary but insufficient given NATO 3.5% core target
Opposing all defence increases with no spending target contradicts the evidence that the threat environment requires higher spending
Strong defence rhetoric supports increased spending but without specifics on how much or how funded
Nuclear deterrent costs crowd out conventional capabilities within the same defence budget
Higher overall spending creates the headroom within which the capital-personnel rebalance can happen
Cheap drone manufacturing enables the technology investment within the rebalanced spending model
Germany overtaking the UK demonstrates that European allies are spending at rates that make the UK gap even larger and more urgent
Spain stating 5% is incompatible with a welfare state is the direct evidence that the £80-130bn gap forces a choice between defence and services
NATO agreeing higher targets in a deteriorating threat environment strengthens the case for maintaining nuclear deterrent as strategic insurance

cross_domain_edges.explanation: Defence spending increases without cutting services depend entirely on the fiscal space created by taxation reform
The £80-130bn total gap is the most important cross-domain finding and defence is its largest component
IFS noting non-defence spending is 'almost flat' proves defence increases directly squeeze NHS and other service funding
Spain's welfare state trade-off framing directly supports the welfare domain's finding that services fail because of fiscal constraint not benefit generosity
Energy security is a defence concern — domestic clean power reduces military exposure to fossil fuel supply chain disruption
